narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, test scores, and plans like whether you'd study full-time or part-time. For you specifically, it estimated a 57% probability that you would fail the bar exam—high enough that the university decided to reject your application to prioritize candidates with stronger predicted odds of success. The decision hinged mostly on three standout factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on this outcome. The biggest push toward predicting failure came from your LSAT score of 25, which is notably below the typical applicant's 36.7 and signaled to the model a higher risk of struggling on the bar. Countering that somewhat was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4th, around the average of 5.53 and strong enough to nudge the prediction toward passing, showing solid later performance. Your Year 1 decile of 1st, however, pulled in the opposite direction toward failure, as it was far above the average 5.71 and highlighted an early academic stumble that the model weighed heavily. Compared to typical students, where over 92% plan full-time study like you opted for part-time, your overall undergrad GPA of 2.4 versus the average 3.21 also played into the broader risk picture, though not as dominantly. This combination made the model see you as riskier than most applicants who pass the bar on their first attempt. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score significantly, as it's the top factor here—many students improve with targeted prep. Strengthening your recent academic decile further through additional coursework could help offset early weaknesses. Exploring full-time study options might align better with successful profiles too. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback, and look into bridge programs or other schools that value your upper-middle background and part-time flexibility. With focused improvements, you can reshape this prediction and strengthen your next application.
